title: Maths and Stats for AI Engineers

slides.title: Vectors and Cosine Similarity
slides.content: In the realm of AI engineering, Vectors are the fundamental mathematical units used to represent complex data in a structured, multi-dimensional space. Whether it's a piece of text converted into an embedding or a set of user behaviors, everything is distilled into a sequence of numbers—a vector. The distance or angle between these vectors is what allows us to mathematically define 'Similarity.' Cosine Similarity, specifically, measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, providing a value between -1 and 1. In our FRAISE architecture, we use this logic to power internal search engines and AI-driven content recommendations. By calculating how 'close' a user's query vector is to our site configuration vectors, we can instantly retrieve the most relevant information with mathematical precision, regardless of the size of the dataset.

studentPrompts: How would you represent a simple 'Site Profile' (e.g., name, color, number of pages) as a 3D vector?
Explain why Cosine Similarity is often preferred over Euclidean distance for high-dimensional text embeddings.
What happens to the Cosine Similarity score if two vectors are perfectly identical vs perfectly opposite?

slides.title: Matrix Transformations
slides.content: Matrix transformations are the mathematical 'Engines' that allow us to manipulate data points through scaling, rotation, and translation in n-dimensional space. In computer graphics and data science, a matrix multiplication is the standard way to transform a vector from one state to another. For an AI engineer, understanding these transformations is vital for processing everything from image data to complex neural network weights. In the FRAISE `SiteBenchmarker.vue`, we might use matrix transformations to scale and position performance data points within a 2D canvas visualization, ensuring that raw metrics are transformed into a legible, interactive UI. By mastering linear transformations, you gain the ability to navigate and manipulate the mathematical fabric of any modern software system, providing the foundation for everything from advanced visual effects to the fundamental operations of deep learning models.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g transform="translate(100,100)"><line x1="-80" y1="0" x2="80" y2="0" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="0" y1="-80" x2="0" y2="80" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="0" y="-50" width="50" height="50" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="0,0 70,-20 50,-90 -20,-70" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/><text x="5" y="-55" fill="#3b82f6" font-size="12" font-family="sans-serif">Original</text><text x="20" y="-95" fill="#ef4444" font-size="12" font-family="sans-serif">Transformed</text></g></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the physical intuition of a 'Matrix' as a function that maps one coordinate system to another.
How would you use a 2x2 matrix to double the height of an element while keeping its width the same?
What is an 'Identity Matrix' and why is it the logical equivalent of the number '1' in matrix multiplication?

slides.title: Normal Distribution and Percentiles
slides.content: Statistical analysis is the cornerstone of professional application performance monitoring. Understanding the 'Normal Distribution' (or Bell Curve) helps us visualize how most users experience our systems, but for high-stakes engineering, the 'Edges' are where the real work begins. We often use 'Percentiles' like P99 (the 99th percentile) to identify the slowest 1% of requests. While the 'Mean' (average) might show a fast system, a high P99 indicates that a small group of users is experiencing extreme lag, which can lead to catastrophic churn. In our `stack-performance-benchmarking.md` guide, we define how to measure and interpret these distributions to ensure that the FRAISE CMS remains lightning-fast for *everyone*, not just the average user. Mastering these statistical concepts allows you to move beyond 'it works on my machine' and into the realm of data-driven, reliable infrastructure management.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="20" y1="180" x2="280" y2="180" stroke="#374151" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M 30 180 Q 80 180 100 100 T 150 20 T 200 100 T 270 180" fill="none" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="150" y1="180" x2="150" y2="20" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-dasharray="5,5"/><text x="135" y="195" font-size="12" font-family="sans-serif" fill="#4b5563">Mean</text><line x1="220" y1="180" x2="220" y2="140" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-dasharray="5,5"/><text x="210" y="195" font-size="12" fill="#ef4444" font-family="sans-serif">P99</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is the 'Average' (Mean) often a misleading metric for web application performance monitoring?
Explain the concept of 'Standard Deviation' and how it measures the 'Spread' or 'Consistency' of your server's response times.
How would you use percentile data to set a 'Service Level Objective' (SLO) for a client's e-commerce site?

slides.title: Dimensionality Reduction (PCA)
slides.content: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a powerful statistical technique used for 'Dimensionality Reduction'—the process of simplifying complex, multi-dimensional data while preserving as much of the original information (variance) as possible. In AI engineering, we often deal with data that has hundreds or thousands of features, making it impossible to visualize and computationally expensive to process. PCA identifies the 'Principal Components'—the directions along which the data varies the most—and projects the data onto these axes. This allows us to reduce a massive 1000-dimensional text embedding into a 2D or 3D point that can be easily visualized in a dashboard or analyzed for patterns. In our `PRACTICAL-BUSINESS-DEVOPS.md` guide, we use PCA concepts to simplify the analysis of thousands of performance metrics, helping engineers find the 'Signal' in the noisy 'Data Storm' of a production environment.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="50" cy="150" r="4" fill="#111827"/><circle cx="70" cy="130" r="4" fill="#111827"/><circle cx="90" cy="110" r="4" fill="#111827"/><circle cx="110" cy="90" r="4" fill="#111827"/><circle cx="130" cy="70" r="4" fill="#111827"/><circle cx="150" cy="50" r="4" fill="#111827"/><circle cx="60" cy="145" r="3" fill="#9ca3af"/><circle cx="85" cy="120" r="3" fill="#9ca3af"/><circle cx="100" cy="105" r="3" fill="#9ca3af"/><circle cx="120" cy="85" r="3" fill="#9ca3af"/><circle cx="140" cy="60" r="3" fill="#9ca3af"/><line x1="30" y1="170" x2="170" y2="30" stroke="#8b5cf6" stroke-width="2"/><text x="120" y="25" fill="#8b5cf6" font-size="12" font-family="sans-serif">Principal Component</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does 'Dimensionality Reduction' help prevent the 'Curse of Dimensionality' in machine learning models?
Explain the relationship between PCA and 'Variance'—why do we want to preserve the directions with the most spread?
Describe a real-world scenario where reducing a 50-feature dataset to a 3-feature dataset would be beneficial.
